Anonymous, 5 hours agof equivalents on mobile phones are simply not true, they are skewed for marketing purposes bec... moreIt could be anything. Lens aperture and sensor size are objective measures, so that should make photos better. Lens quality is not something you see on the specsheet, so that could be part of it. However, time and time again, these Chinese companies have produced cleaner photos. Smaller sensors and dimmer lenses typically require more post processing. That said, the difference isn't as big as some people make it out to be.

Anonymous, 21 hours agoThe 3.5x (or 3x if it is a 70mm lens like previously rumoured) module is 1/1.28" (Omnivis... moref equivalents on mobile phones are simply not true, they are skewed for marketing purposes because they are just equivalents so they just get away with it no matter what they claim to lower those numbers down especially on big sensors.

IceColdPhone, 21 hours agoI mean bigger sensor will be better? thats the reason. I am happy to get a 3x tele thats bette... moreNo, not at all since those big telephoto sensors in Chinese Ultras rely so heavily on postprocessing so I can't really tell the optical difference. The actual physical difference between a telephoto module housing 1.4" and twice as smaller 2.0" sensor is drastic in every way yet the actual image difference quality is negligible. First of all, big sensors require proper lens stack in order to receive light they need and that's very expensive to do. Smaller sensors don't require quality lens components as they need less light. All Chinese Ultras claim very fast glass but in reality its iffy. Toggle between Pro Photo and Pro Video at native ISO in dim environment to see how video is a lot dimmer than photo

This is all very well, but as only the iPhone can make decent Panoramas with its Ultrawide lens, all Android phones (like Seamus Android, Crackerjack fans!) except just possibly a Samsung or two are NO USE!! I mean , what is the point of a machine with several great sensor/lens combinations if you then deprive it of the means to fully use those same lenses. 
Can you, in a confined space make a perfectly corrected panorama of a full circle turn with your 14mm lens? If you cannot you are back in the Lost Ark with the LG g7+ from 2018, which STILL beats all modern Androids for pans with its 16mm lens, because it can at least make them. 
 As for the GSMArena staff continuing to behave as though the sweep panorama no longer is part of human progress, ignoring this important new 21st century means of expression, its like old friends who now wont talk to me because I photograph in colour...

jiyen235, 13 hours agothe find x7 ultra looks more sharpened if you look closer but yes the find x8 ultra employs be... moreFor the UWA camera, Find X8 Ultra applies more sharpening than Find X7 Ultra does. In the samples you linked to, you can clearly see that the edges of the table and the objects on the table are showing far more noticeable ringing artifacts. Find X7 Ultra slightly grainier around the corners and slightly softer in the centre, but maintains a more natural look here. 
 
"Whatever the case, considering the size difference the find x8 ultra should lose completely" 
Nope. Find X8 Ultra's camera simply uses more effective noise reduction algorithms and demosaicing, which mostly make up for the reduction in smaller pixel pitch. I swear 100% that Find X9 Ultra's UWA camera will be noticeably better than that on Find X8 Ultra, since the latter would be at an advantage in BOTH image processing and the sheer sensor size. Oppo would have to seriously screw something up if they somehow manage to make X9 Ultra perform worse here. 
 
"compare X100 Pro vs Ultra, the Pro has an older JN1 sensor and it's still surprisingly competitive." 
X100 Ultra's UWA captures a slightly wider field of view and has a smaller F-stop, meaning it captures only 12% more light than X100 Pro's UWA, yet it manages to retain significantly finer details if you look at both phones' old balcony sample shots. In fact, in broad daylight, the X100 Pro *should* be sharper across the frame since it simply has more pixels per field of view to render the scene. If you want to refer to the existing samples, at least make sure that they support your case rather than shoot you in the back.

jiyen235, 15 hours agofind x7 ultra and vivo x100 ultra use it as UW cams. Although they're big sensor UW cams ... moreIf you see any "blurry" details they are attributed to either a lack of sharpening, less mature demosaicing algorithms, or a lower quality lens. They are both 50MP sensors that bin down to 12.5MP, so when they are given theoretically perfect lenses and an identical image processing pipeline to back it up, they should resolve the same level of detail. In the most ideal lighting conditions, the only property of a sensor that could affect the resolution of the image is its pixel count as well how colour filters are arranged (You could argue that pixel pitch affects diffraction, but that's more to do with the physical aperture size and the diameter of the airy disk it produces). 
 
In your example, Find X8 Ultra is employing more sharpening and noise reduction to give the photo an appearance of a clean image, but it's actually losing quite a lot of details towards the edges and corners of the frame. "Better denoise" is literally coming from the image processing part, not from the sensor.

Anonymous, 21 hours agoI don't think 10x is not the problem here cause sensor is probably compact. Its the 3.5x ... moreThe 3.5x (or 3x if it is a 70mm lens like previously rumoured) module is 1/1.28" (Omnivision OV52A) if I'm not mistaken. That's the same size as the current largest sensor in a phone periscope in the Huawei Pura 80 Ultra. This one is brighter, too, if the rumours are true - f/2.2 vs f/2.4. Light intake is very important, though - it's the one thing phones have been battling since the dawn of time. The 10x module is rumoured to be an ISOCELL JNL sensor, which appears to be a custom ISOCELL JN5, since most of the specs, according to rumours, are a match, including the 50MP 1/2.76" size. It is mated to a 230mm f/3.5 lens as per leaks, which together puts it far ahead of the 10MP 1/3.52" 230mm f/4.9 setup of the S23 Ultra. I would personally take the Oppo over the vivo this time, where it was the opposite case last year. The 1/1.28" ultrawide is cool, yes, but requires a massive camera bump to maintain distortions, plus in my opinion, the zoom on the vivo is not as good as the Oppo, at least on paper. vivo's multispectral camera is a higher 5MP resolution, as opposed to the leaked 3.2MP here, but I wouldn't be swayed by that alone.
